skills.skill: Properties of Wave Pulses and Waves
skills.prompt: This topic covers properties of wave pulses and waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that waves transfer energy without transferring matter. A wave pulse is a single disturbance; a continuous wave is a repeated periodic disturbance. Transverse waves have particle displacement perpendicular to wave propagation (electromagnetic waves, waves on strings). Longitudinal waves have particle displacement parallel to propagation (sound waves). Key properties include amplitude (maximum displacement), wavelength (distance between consecutive identical points), frequency (oscillations per second), and wave speed (v = f*lambda). Wave speed depends on the medium, not on frequency or amplitude. Students must classify wave types and calculate wave properties. Connect to superposition, standing waves, and electromagnetic waves. AP exam note: wave property relationships are foundational. Vocabulary: transverse wave, longitudinal wave, amplitude, wavelength, frequency, wave speed, pulse, medium.

This topic covers periodic waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that periodic waves are generated by periodic sources and have constant frequency, wavelength, and amplitude in a uniform medium. The wave equation v = f*lambda relates speed, frequency, and wavelength. Period T = 1/f is the time for one complete cycle. For waves on a string: v = sqrt(F_tension/mu), where mu is linear mass density. For sound in air: v depends on temperature (approximately 343 m/s at 20 degrees C). Students must interpret wave diagrams showing displacement vs. position (wavelength visible) and displacement vs. time (period visible), and distinguish between the two graph types. Connect to wave speed, standing waves, and musical instruments. AP exam note: interpreting wave graphs and applying v = f*lambda are core skills. Vocabulary: periodic wave, frequency, wavelength, period, wave speed, wave equation, displacement graph, continuous wave.

skills.skill: Boundary Behavior of Waves and Polarization
skills.prompt: This topic covers boundary behavior of waves and polarization for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that when a wave reaches a boundary between media, part is reflected and part is transmitted. A wave reflecting from a fixed end (denser medium) inverts; from a free end (less dense medium) it reflects upright. When entering a denser medium, wavelength decreases (frequency stays constant, speed decreases). Polarization applies only to transverse waves: a polarizer transmits only the component of oscillation along its transmission axis. Malus's law: I = I_0*cos^2(theta), where theta is the angle between the polarization direction and the transmission axis. Unpolarized light passing through a polarizer loses half its intensity. Students must analyze boundary reflections and calculate transmitted intensity through polarizers. AP exam note: polarization and boundary behavior are tested. Vocabulary: reflection, transmission, fixed end, free end, inversion, polarization, polarizer, Malus's law, transverse wave.

This topic covers electromagnetic waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that electromagnetic (EM) waves are transverse waves consisting of oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation. All EM waves travel at the speed of light in vacuum: c = 3.0 x 10^8 m/s. The EM spectrum ranges from radio waves (lowest frequency, longest wavelength) through microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, to gamma rays (highest frequency, shortest wavelength). EM waves require no medium and carry energy proportional to frequency: E = hf. Students must relate frequency, wavelength, and speed for EM waves and order them on the spectrum. Connect to photon energy, spectroscopy, and wave-particle duality. AP exam note: EM spectrum ordering and c = f*lambda calculations are tested. Vocabulary: electromagnetic wave, speed of light, electric field, magnetic field, EM spectrum, transverse, photon, frequency.

This topic covers the Doppler effect for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that the Doppler effect is the change in observed frequency (and wavelength) of a wave due to relative motion between the source and observer. When the source and observer approach each other, the observed frequency increases (higher pitch for sound, blue shift for light). When they move apart, the observed frequency decreases (lower pitch, red shift). For sound: f_observed = f_source * (v +/- v_observer) / (v -/+ v_source), where v is the wave speed in the medium. The effect depends on the velocity relative to the medium. For light, the relativistic Doppler formula applies but is not required for AP Physics 2. Students must predict frequency shifts and solve Doppler problems qualitatively and quantitatively. Connect to wave properties, sound, and astronomy. AP exam note: qualitative Doppler effect reasoning is commonly tested. Vocabulary: Doppler effect, frequency shift, blue shift, red shift, approaching, receding, observer, source.

This topic covers wave interference and standing waves for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that when two waves overlap, they superpose: constructive interference occurs when waves are in phase (crests align, amplitudes add) and destructive interference occurs when waves are out of phase (crest meets trough, amplitudes cancel). Standing waves form when two waves of equal frequency and amplitude travel in opposite directions. Standing waves have nodes (zero displacement, destructive interference) and antinodes (maximum displacement, constructive interference). For a string fixed at both ends: harmonics occur at f_n = n*v/(2L). For an open pipe: same formula. For a closed pipe (one end closed): f_n = n*v/(4L), n = odd integers only. Students must identify harmonics and calculate resonant frequencies. AP exam note: standing wave patterns and harmonic calculations are heavily tested. Vocabulary: interference, constructive, destructive, standing wave, node, antinode, harmonic, resonance, fundamental frequency.

This topic covers diffraction for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.7). Students should understand that diffraction is the bending and spreading of waves around obstacles or through openings. Significant diffraction occurs when the opening size is comparable to the wavelength. Single-slit diffraction produces a central maximum wider than the slit with dimmer secondary maxima on either side. The condition for minima in single-slit diffraction: a*sin(theta) = m*lambda, where a is the slit width and m = 1, 2, 3, .... Narrower slits produce wider diffraction patterns. The central maximum contains most of the energy and is twice as wide as secondary maxima. Students must predict diffraction patterns and calculate positions of minima. Connect to interference, wave properties, and the wave nature of light. AP exam note: single-slit diffraction patterns and calculations are tested. Vocabulary: diffraction, single slit, central maximum, secondary maximum, minimum, slit width, wavelength, spreading.

This topic covers double-slit interference and diffraction gratings for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.8). Students should understand that in Young's double-slit experiment, coherent light passing through two slits produces an interference pattern of bright and dark fringes. Constructive interference (bright fringes): d*sin(theta) = m*lambda (m = 0, 1, 2, ...). Destructive interference (dark fringes): d*sin(theta) = (m + 1/2)*lambda. The fringe spacing on a screen: delta-y = lambda*L/d. A diffraction grating has many slits and produces sharper, more defined maxima at the same angular positions. Greater slit number produces sharper peaks. Students must calculate fringe positions, angular separations, and wavelengths from interference data. Connect to wave nature of light, single-slit diffraction, and spectroscopy. AP exam note: double-slit calculations and diffraction grating analysis are heavily tested. Vocabulary: double slit, interference pattern, bright fringe, dark fringe, path difference, diffraction grating, order, coherent light.

This topic covers thin-film interference for AP Physics 2 students (CED: Unit 6, Topic 6.9). Students should understand that when light reflects from the top and bottom surfaces of a thin transparent film, the two reflected beams can interfere constructively or destructively depending on the film thickness, wavelength, and refractive indices. A phase shift of 180 degrees (half wavelength) occurs upon reflection from a denser medium (higher n). The effective wavelength in the film is lambda_film = lambda_vacuum/n_film. For constructive interference (accounting for phase shifts): 2*n*t = (m + 1/2)*lambda when one phase shift occurs, or 2*n*t = m*lambda when zero or two phase shifts occur. Applications include antireflection coatings, soap bubbles, and oil films on water. Students must determine conditions for constructive and destructive interference in thin films. AP exam note: thin-film problems require careful tracking of phase shifts. Vocabulary: thin-film interference, phase shift, optical path length, constructive, destructive, antireflection coating, refractive index.
